assertions: When `.zoto/spec-system/config.yml` is absent at the repository root, the command does not spawn `zoto-spec-judge` and does not synthesise a replacement config file.
The surfaced text is exactly: Spec System is not initialised. Run `/z-spec-init` first to create `.zoto/spec-system/config.yml`.
After `/z-spec-judge`, the manifest records the `z-spec-judge` attempt and shows no completed hand-off to `zoto-spec-judge` because execution halted during precondition checking.

primitive_analysis.summary: Exercises `/z-spec-judge` across the missing-config abort, repository-wide judging, directory- and file-scoped spec judging with rubric-linked verdicts, and a follow-up turn that accepts spec-only fixes gated by askQuestion while logging applied changes in the assessment note.

cases.expected_output: The invocation stops before any spawned judge runs, emitting only the mandated spec-system initialisation error and leaving no new assessment markdown behind.

assertions: After `/z-spec-judge` with no arguments, a new `zoto-spec-judge` subagent starts in isolation and receives empty `$ARGUMENTS` for the full-repo pass.
The subagent executes through the `zoto-judge-spec` skill while walking agents, skills, commands, hooks, automated tests, scripts, and configuration layout.
A markdown file is written to `{specsDir}/assessment-repo-[yyyymmdd].md` using the repo’s configured `{specsDir}` segment.
The written analysis visibly covers completeness, feasibility, structure, specificity, risk awareness, and convention compliance before stating any readiness opinion.
After `/z-spec-judge`, the manifest MUST record at least two targets tying the palette command to the repository assessment outcome.

primitive_analysis.summary: Exercises `/z-spec-judge` across the missing-config abort, repository-wide judging, directory- and file-scoped spec judging with rubric-linked verdicts, and a follow-up turn that accepts spec-only fixes gated by askQuestion while logging applied changes in the assessment note.

cases.expected_output: A fresh repository assessment report appears under the configured specs directory using the assessment-repo date-stamped filename pattern and summarises health across the six mandated review axes.

assertions: After `/z-spec-judge specs/20260403-feature-name`, the spawned subagent receives that directory token verbatim via `$ARGUMENTS`.
The workflow discovers the `spec-*.md` index under that folder, loads associated subtask files, and audits the dependency graph for inconsistencies.
The deliverable lands inside `specs/20260403-feature-name/` following the `assessment-[feature-name]-[yyyymmdd].md` naming convention described for directory arguments.
Scoring guidance is applied so approvals align with scores at or above 4.0, conditional findings sit between 3.0 and 3.9, and rejection covers scores below 3.0 when steering toward `/z-spec-execute`.
After `/z-spec-judge`, the manifest MUST record the command plus delegation metadata that ties the run to that spec directory rather than the whole workspace.

primitive_analysis.summary: Exercises `/z-spec-judge` across the missing-config abort, repository-wide judging, directory- and file-scoped spec judging with rubric-linked verdicts, and a follow-up turn that accepts spec-only fixes gated by askQuestion while logging applied changes in the assessment note.

cases.expected_output: The judge ingests the directory’s spec index plus linked subtasks, critiques dependencies and risk, emits a dated assessment beside that tree, and frames verdict language using the approve, conditional, and reject score bands.

assertions: After `/z-spec-judge specs/20260403-feature-name/spec-feature-name-20260403.md`, judgement work uses that explicit index path when resolving the spec package.
Report output remains co-located with the spec directory housing the named index instead of drifting into unrelated product modules.
Passive review inspects implementation areas only for verification and does not rewrite application source files before any optional fix offer.
After `/z-spec-judge`, the manifest MUST echo the precise file argument so traceability matches the operator-supplied path.

primitive_analysis.summary: Exercises `/z-spec-judge` across the missing-config abort, repository-wide judging, directory- and file-scoped spec judging with rubric-linked verdicts, and a follow-up turn that accepts spec-only fixes gated by askQuestion while logging applied changes in the assessment note.

cases.expected_output: Assessment is anchored on the supplied index file, keeps generated notes within the same spec directory hierarchy, and confines automatic edits later to spec artefacts only.

assertions: For directory-scoped spec assessments the judge surfaces actionable findings and issues `askQuestion` before altering any spec index, subtask file, or dependency graph artifact.
When the operator accepts, modifications stay limited to spec markdown and dependency metadata—runtime service code and libraries remain untouched.
The assessment markdown is revised to spell out which automated fixes executed so reviewers see a complete audit trail.
After `/z-spec-judge`, the manifest MUST capture the `askQuestion` prompt prior to logging any write operations against spec paths.

primitive_analysis.summary: Exercises `/z-spec-judge` across the missing-config abort, repository-wide judging, directory- and file-scoped spec judging with rubric-linked verdicts, and a follow-up turn that accepts spec-only fixes gated by askQuestion while logging applied changes in the assessment note.

cases.expected_output: The spec assessment finishes, askQuestion prompts whether to apply structured fixes, accepted changes touch solely spec-side documents, and the assessment file records which remediations landed.
follow_ups: Proceed with the remediation: tighten the listed subtasks, repair the dependency edges, and refresh the assessment appendix to list the edits you applied.
